glosses.phrase: so conjunctive to my life and soul
as the star moves not but in his sphere
glosses.definition: as a planet cannot move outside its orbit

glosses.phrase: in forgery of shapes and tricks

annotations.title: The plot-making scene: Claudius at his most Machiavellian
annotations.body: The King's exchange with Laertes is a masterclass in manipulation. Claudius does not propose the murder directly; he asks leading questions ('Was your father dear to you?') and lets Laertes arrive at the solution Claudius already has in mind. The staged fencing match, the unblunted rapier, the anointing with a 'mountebank's' poison, and the backup plan of a poisoned cup — all are introduced as if Laertes is the improviser and the King merely the sounding board. Shakespeare's Claudius is not the cardboard villain of the revenge-tragedy tradition. He is the political intelligence that revenge tragedy requires to generate its catastrophes, and his technique here is nearer Iago's than to any Senecan stereotype.

annotations.title: Gertrude's elegy for Ophelia
annotations.body: The report of Ophelia's drowning is one of the most famous speeches in English poetry, and also one of the most theatrically strange. It is delivered in past tense, as if by an eyewitness, by a queen who was certainly not there. The language — 'crow-flowers, nettles, daisies, and long purples' — is botanically precise and visually suspended. Critics from Coleridge onward have noted that the speech behaves less like a report than like a tableau. The pastoral mode (weeping willow, garlands, mermaid-like singing) softens the violence; Ophelia 'dies' into a painting before she actually dies. Tennyson and Millais would later build their Ophelia on these very lines. What Gertrude does not say — whether the death was accident or suicide — the gravediggers will debate in 5.1.

annotations.title: The poison plot and the logic of the foil
annotations.body: Laertes's proposed poisoned rapier-tip is the scene's ethical nadir. Under Renaissance honor-code, a duel was a ritual combat that tested fate and skill; poisoning a foil turns the ritual into a secret assassination while preserving the ceremonial surface. The word 'unction,' applied to the poison, is theologically loaded — it is the word for the sacrament of anointing, here reversed into damnation. Shakespeare is noting a specific corruption of chivalry. The fallback plan — a poisoned cup, should the sword fail — represents the total betrayal of the duel's pretense. When the plan turns on its makers in Act 5, the rhetorical closure is near-surgical: poisons kill their compounders.

trials.prompt: How does Claudius convert Laertes's revenge into his own instrument?
options: By offering Laertes the throne.
By leading Laertes to propose a treacherous fencing match that Claudius in fact designed.
By forging a letter that incriminates Hamlet.
By locking Laertes in a dungeon until he agrees.

trials.prompt: What does Laertes propose to do to the rapier for the duel?
options: Polish it with a secret rust remover.
Anoint its tip with a poison bought from a mountebank, so that even a scratch will kill.
Weigh it down with lead to favor him in the strike.
Have it blessed by a priest so God will favor his cause.

trials.prompt: What backup plan does Claudius propose if Laertes's poisoned rapier fails?
options: A second swordsman hidden in the gallery.
A poisoned cup of wine for Hamlet to drink 'for the nonce.'
A carriage accident on the return from the duel.
A hidden pistol behind the arras.

trials.prompt: What peculiar aspect of Gertrude's speech reporting Ophelia's drowning has puzzled critics?
options: It is spoken in prose rather than verse.
She describes the death in precise, pastoral detail as if she had witnessed it, though she cannot have been present.
It contains anachronistic references to modern flowers.
It is delivered in Latin.

trials.prompt: What does the metaphor of a 'tree' in Gertrude's speech — the 'envious sliver' that breaks — suggest about Ophelia's death?
options: That the death is partly a natural accident and not clearly a suicide.
That Ophelia was deliberately pushed by an unseen assailant.
That the tree itself is a metaphor for King Hamlet's spirit.
That the willow carries biblical weight as a symbol of mourning.
